findings.title: Standardized weekly content box per model
findings.claim: Standardizing creator output into a fixed weekly content format lets an agency scale production across many models with minimal model and founder time.
findings.what_they_do: Hive Media (faceless francis interview) requires two 'sets' per model per week, each composed of part A casual SFW realism content, part B sexy social content, and part C a sexting set or PPV, so every model drops into the same box that feeds both the marketing and the sales machines on four-to-six hours of model time.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a mature, data-driven SOCIAL ideation pipeline (§1) but no standardized OF vault/PPV content format (§2 THIN). Partial gap on type-2 content.
findings.recommendation: Define a standard weekly content-set format for Palm creators (SFW realism / sexy social / vault-or-PPV) so vault production scales the same way the social pipeline already does.

sources.quote: every week the models have to deliver two sets. A set consists of A, B, and C

findings.title: Spreadsheet/Airtable backend at scale
findings.claim: Running account and content operations off spreadsheet-style databases with front-end interfaces is the pragmatic backbone for managing accounts at scale.
findings.what_they_do: Hive Media stores 10,000+ social accounts (proxy, 2FA, status, creation date) in Excel/Sheets as the database with front-end interfaces on top, updated automatically by bots, and explicitly says Airtable is just a prettier version of the same database approach.
findings.vs_us: Palm already runs on Airtable (HQ + Content Ops bases) with a Next.js admin front end (§0), so Palm is aligned with this best practice. Validation, not a gap.
findings.recommendation: Keep the Airtable-as-database plus admin-UI pattern; ensure account-status fields (proxy, ban status, creation date) are tracked and auto-updated as rigorously as Hive does.

sources.quote: the only thing better than a spreadsheet might be a slightly prettier spreadsheet in something like Air Table

findings.title: Provision reserve accounts for burn
findings.claim: Assuming a baseline account-ban burn rate and pre-provisioning reserve accounts is required to run social acquisition at scale without interruption.
findings.what_they_do: Hive Media issues 200 accounts per platform per model (about 100 active immediately, 100 in reserve), runs a daily script using public profile views to auto-detect bans, and accepts that even fully organic operations always carry some burn.
findings.vs_us: Palm runs hardware-isolated AI account warmup (§3/§4 MATURE) but has no documented reserve-account or burn-rate provisioning model. Gap.
findings.recommendation: Adopt a reserve-account buffer per AI persona plus an automated daily ban-detection check, sized to an expected burn rate, so a ban never stalls a creator's funnel.

sources.quote: 100 that become active immediately when they start then 100 for reserve... there's always the risk of having some bans

findings.title: Build so the founder can disappear
findings.claim: Heavy supervision layers and clear workflows let the founder step away while the business keeps running, which is the real test of a scaled agency.
findings.what_they_do: Hive Media (300 employees, 60 models) layers supervisors into category managers into shift/market managers per department, and the founder says if he disappeared for six months the company would still run profitably.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a thin org with many decisions through the founder and no org chart or scaling plan (§10 THIN). Direct gap.
findings.recommendation: Build a supervision layer (team leads / managers) per Palm function with explicit workflows so the founder can step out without the operation stalling.

sources.quote: if I disappear for 6 months right now, will my company grow? Maybe not exponentially, but it will run

findings.title: Steer on CAC:LTV and margin, not followers
findings.claim: Allocating resources by cost-per-acquisition versus LTV and target margin, rather than by follower counts or a raw LTV number, is how a scaled agency decides where to invest.
findings.what_they_do: Hive Media tracks free and paid LTV over 7 and 30 days against a roughly $1 Reddit CAC, targets a 50% margin on base traffic (accepting 20-30% on low-effort purchased traffic), and color-codes models so investment flows to the highest CAC:LTV returns; the founder calls 'what is a good LTV' the wrong question.
findings.vs_us: Palm collects fan/whale data but leadership reporting is in-flight and there is no standard CAC:LTV or margin-per-creator view (§9 PARTIAL, §7 THIN). Gap.
findings.recommendation: Adopt a per-creator CAC:LTV dashboard with a target margin, and allocate posting/acquisition spend by that ratio instead of by follower vanity metrics.

sources.quote: we are always monitoring free LTV and paid LTV over a 7-day and 30-day period
what is a good LTV... it's just the wrong question... what we actually care about is cac to ltv

findings.title: AI scores chat transcripts daily
findings.claim: An AI model trained on past chats can read every chatter's transcripts daily and surface buy-signals, missed offers, and coaching feedback that human supervisors cannot cover at scale.
findings.what_they_do: Hive Media pulls Infloww message-board exports into an LLM trained on two years of their own chats to produce daily per-chatter reports (interest/buy signals vs offers sent, left-on-read counts, missed opportunities, KPIs vs peers) because even five supervisors cannot watch 30-40 chatters live.
findings.vs_us: Chatting is outsourced and Palm owns no QA loop (§8 OUTSOURCED), but AI chatting is an active Palm project. Direct build-toward for that roadmap.
findings.recommendation: For Palm's AI-chatting project, design an AI transcript-QA layer that scores every conversation daily (buy-signal capture, offers sent, misses) and feeds coaching back to chatters.

sources.quote: an AI LLM model that we train... based on two years worth of data of our own chats... how many interest signals... opportunities they missed

findings.title: Give every model a written character
findings.claim: Building a written character/persona for each creator and aligning all content to it raises LTV by enabling a parasocial relationship rather than generic thirst traps.
findings.what_they_do: Hive Media sits each new model down to create an in-depth character sheet (occupation, personality, persona, backstory) and aligns outfits, scenes, and PPVs to it, deliberately targeting spending demographics by the model's look so chatters can monetize a fleshed-out character.
findings.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline drives content from viral inspo data (§1) but there is no documented per-creator character/persona layer tying content to a sales story. Gap on branding/type-2.
findings.recommendation: Add a per-creator character sheet (persona, backstory, target fan demographic) that both content and the chat team work from, so content ladders into monetization.

sources.quote: you're actually a character with a story and depth... build a better parasocial relationship with them

findings.title: Reddit needs manual niche targeting
findings.claim: Reddit traffic must be run manually with niche-matched content and captions per subreddit because, unlike IG/X, the poster is effectively the algorithm choosing the audience.
findings.what_they_do: Hive Media keeps 100 manual Reddit VAs (each issued a physical phone within four weeks) matching niche content and captioning to specific subreddits while fully automating IG/X/Threads; Reddit free subs cost $0.25-$1 and return $4-$8.
findings.vs_us: Palm's traffic/distribution strategy (Reddit, paid, mass-DM) is undocumented with no standing playbook (§5 GAP). Direct gap.
findings.recommendation: Build a manual Reddit playbook with niche-matched subreddit targeting and per-subreddit captioning, tracking cost-per-free-sub against LTV.

sources.quote: On Reddit, you are the algorithm essentially. You are determining which subreddits
cost per acquisition of a Reddit subscriber is anywhere from 25 cents to a dollar... getting anywhere from $4 to $8 a return

findings.title: Rapport, transition, then sell
findings.claim: Every chat should build rapport and let the customer initiate the sexual turn before selling, never opening with the sale.
findings.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard trains chatters on a fixed framework (general conversation/rapport, transition to a sexual conversation, then dirty talk leading into PPV) and lets the customer initiate so the model holds the power position; habibi similarly builds rapport and conserves energy rather than blasting horny messages or mass-message bundles.
findings.vs_us: Chatting is outsourced; Palm does not own script flow (§8 OUTSOURCED). Build-toward for the in-house and AI chat roadmap. Cross-creator consensus (Luca + habibi).
findings.recommendation: Adopt a mandated rapport-then-transition-then-sell chat framework as the backbone script when chatting comes in-house or is automated.

sources.quote: Step One is General conversation building report... step two is transitioning to a more sexual conversation
conserving your energy you should never be throwing all of your energy away

findings.title: Make the fan chase to hold power
findings.claim: Letting the fan initiate and withholding rather than over-giving keeps the model in the power position and increases buying.
findings.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard plays hot-and-cold and sends non-nude teasers so the customer initiates, putting the chatter in the power position; habibi withholds (tells a fan asking for photos 'no, you're going to have to wait') to drive desire before selling.
findings.vs_us: Outsourced chatting; Palm owns no objection/power-dynamic playbook (§8 OUTSOURCED). Build-toward. Cross-creator consensus (Luca + habibi).
findings.recommendation: Encode push-pull and let-the-fan-initiate tactics into the in-house chatting SOP rather than leaving tone to the vendor.

sources.quote: we always want to let the customer initiate... we are the ones that are in the power position
even if a guy says hey can you send me photos of your tits you just say simply no you're going to have to wait

findings.title: Use the fan's name, kill mass-messages
findings.claim: Personalizing chats with the fan's name and avoiding generic mass-message bundle blasts is a high-leverage, underused driver of spend and LTV.
findings.what_they_do: Habibi stresses using the fan's actual name instead of 'babe' (people respond to their own name) and refuses mass-message bundle blasts and odd-number 'flea market' pricing, taking a new account from $33 to $500+ days with one-to-one personalized chatting.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM (§9 PARTIAL) but personalization data and tone live with outsourced chatters (§8). Build-toward plus owning the data.
findings.recommendation: Ensure Palm's own per-fan CRM captures the name and interests, and bar generic mass-message bundle blasts, so personalization survives any chatting-vendor change and feeds future AI chat.

sources.quote: people get aroused by their name... it's very underutilized even by chatters
we were sending no stupid... bundle deals... I don't advise mass messages at all

findings.title: First PPV is cheap to build the habit
findings.claim: Pricing the first PPV low (roughly $5-$20) exists to get the fan used to spending, not to make money on that sale.
findings.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard caps the first PPV at $5-$20, never higher, to get the customer used to the habit of buying on the platform before laddering up; habibi tests a brand-new buyer with a small first buy (e.g. $9) to read his boundaries.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model; pricing is set loosely by the outsourced agency (§7 THIN). Direct gap. Cross-creator consensus (Luca + habibi).
findings.recommendation: Define a standard low entry-PPV band ($5-$20) whose explicit goal is first-purchase conversion, separate from profit PPVs.

sources.quote: price the very first pbv between $5 to $20 let's say not go any higher than that... get them used to spending
the last one nine bucks this was a first buy for him... I wanted to test his boundaries

findings.title: Ladder PPV prices upward in sequence
findings.claim: Selling via an escalating price ladder rather than one high ask raises total spend while keeping each step psychologically easy.
findings.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard runs a default ladder of 15, 25, 45, 65, then 100-120, and warns that hitting a warmed-up fan with a single $80 PPV makes him leave for no money, where the same total is extracted reliably in small rising steps.
findings.vs_us: No formal price ladder at Palm (§7 THIN). Direct gap.
findings.recommendation: Publish a standard Palm PPV ladder with rising steps and trigger conditions, instead of single high asks.

sources.quote: We'll start at 15 we'll then go to 25 we'll then go to 45 we'll then go to 65 and... 100 or 120

findings.title: Price dynamically off purchase history
findings.claim: Setting price per fan from their purchase history, backing off after a no and pushing higher for proven whales, beats one fixed price for all.
findings.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard uses dynamic pricing read from each subscriber's unlock history via tools like Infloww: if a fan stalled at $65 he offers 17/27/47/59 next time, but if a fan will pay $150-$200 for what others pay $20 for he sells at that willingness, and he keeps explicit content premium-priced so perceived value stays high.
findings.vs_us: No per-fan price discipline or perceived-value rule at Palm (§7 THIN). Direct gap.
findings.recommendation: Adopt a dynamic per-fan pricing rule keyed to unlock history, plus a floor that keeps explicit content premium-priced to protect perceived value.

sources.quote: you pick a different selling strategy for every single subscriber
the low price might make him not want to buy instead of buy... keep the content premium priced
